scenario_title: add CNAME and add another type at the same owner in one UPDATE
test_rationale: The zone starts with a harmless A RR at cname1.campus.edu. so the UPDATE can meaningfully request a CNAME plus another RR at the same owner in one message. This directly exercises the protocol gap around exclusivity enforcement and error mapping for a single UPDATE that violates RFC 2181's CNAME rule. The follow-up queries on CNAME, TXT, and A make the resulting owner state observable regardless of whether the server rejects the update outright or partially applies one of the additions.

group_1.behavior: Rejects the UPDATE or applies it in a way that leaves only the original A RRset and no CNAME/TXT at the owner.
group_1.post_state: cname1.campus.edu. remains with A only; CNAME and TXT are absent.

group_2.behavior: Accepts the UPDATE and normalizes or partially applies it so that the owner ends up with one of the requested additions surviving.
group_2.post_state: cname1.campus.edu. shows either CNAME or TXT, or a different exclusivity resolution than BIND.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2181 §10.1 forbids a CNAME from coexisting with any other data at the same owner name, but RFC 2136 §3.4.3 only defines the add operation and does not specify how a server must map or order errors when a single UPDATE message requests both a CNAME and a non-CNAME RR at that owner. That leaves room for implementations to reject the whole UPDATE, partially apply it, or enforce exclusivity in different orders.
